content:
To maintain full control over data, security, and customization, I chose to self-host the application on an Ubuntu Server using WordPress.

2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The deployment process presented several technical challenges that required systematic troubleshooting. This article shares the key issues encountered, their resolutions, and a complete guide to help others successfully set up WordPress on Ubuntu.
Initial Challenges
Network Connectivity and DNS Resolution
After a fresh Ubuntu Server installation, domain name resolution failed. Commands such as nslookup google.com timed out. Investigation revealed that the configured public DNS servers (8.8.8.8 and 1.1.1.1) were blocked by the institutional network.
Resolution Steps:

• Verified network interface configuration with ip addr and routing with ip route.

• • Confirmed basic connectivity via ping to the gateway.

• • Updated the Netplan configuration in /etc/netplan/00-installer-config.yaml to use the institution’s DNS servers (102.220.200.146 and 102.220.200.147).

• • Applied changes using sudo netplan generate, sudo netplan apply, and restarted the resolver service.

• This restored full internet access, enabling package downloads and further installation.
MariaDB Database Initialization

• The next major obstacle occurred during MariaDB 11.8 installation. The service would not start, reporting permission errors and missing system tables (e.g., mysql.plugin does not exist).
Resolution Steps:

• Detailed verification of directory permissions, ownership, filesystem mounts, and AppArmor profiles confirmed no underlying access issues. The root cause was that the system database had not been initialized in MariaDB’s default data directory (/var/lib/mariadb).
Solution:
“sudo mariadb-install-db — user=mysql — datadir=/var/lib/mariadb”
“sudo systemctl start mariadb”
“sudo systemctl enable mariadb”
Complete WordPress Deployment Process
With connectivity and the database backend resolved, the full setup proceeded as follows:

• Install LAMP Stack Components
“sudo apt update”
“sudo apt install apache2 php php-mysql php-curl php-gd php-mbstring php-xml php-zip libapache2-mod-php -y”
2. Configure MariaDB for WordPress
Create a dedicated database and user with appropriate privileges.
3. Install WordPress
Download the latest version, extract files to /var/www/html/, and set correct ownership for the web server user.
4. Configuration
Copy and edit wp-config.php with database credentials. Enable Apache rewrite module and restart the service.
5. Final Installation
Complete the setup through the web-based installer.
Key Takeaways
Self-hosting WordPress on Ubuntu provides significant advantages in control and flexibility but requires attention to system-specific details, particularly with newer package versions. Documenting each troubleshooting step proved invaluable.
Ndugu is now operational and focused on addressing real needs in community group management. The experience reinforced the importance of methodical problem-solving in server administration.
For those planning a similar deployment, I recommend preparing for common pitfalls in networking and database initialization. A companion technical guide with full commands is available
